content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 24, 1993

Paul E. Simpson
Board of Supervisors
127 Lake Almanor West Drive
Chester, CA 96020

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-92-600

Dear Mr. Simpson:

This letter is in response to your request for informal
assistance¹ regarding your duties and responsibilities under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").²

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prevent you
from providing your engineering services to a client who is the
seller of property when the purchaser of the same property appears
before you as a member of the Board of Supervisors for approval of
development plans?

CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not prevent
you or your engineering firm from serving the seller. However,
you are required to disqualify yourself from participating in
decisions that concern the purchaser of the property, if the
seller is source of income to you or your engineering firm, and if
it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a
material financial effect on you, your firm, or the seller.

___________________________

1 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

2 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-600
Page 2

FACTS

You were appointed to the Plumas County Board of Supervisors
in February of 1991. You are also a Consulting Civil Engineer and
Land Surveyor and have a small limited engineering practice. You
have served several major clients over the period of 30 years.

In 1984, your engineering firm was retained by a landowner
regarding a general plan revision and zoning of property. In
1991, your client sold about one-half of the property with a
condition that any land development plans obtain your client's
prior approval. Your firm was again retained to assist the client
in the review and approval process. You have received
compensation in the amount of approximately $5,000 from the
landowner within the previous twelve months.

As a county supervisor, you are now in a position to approve
the land development plans of the purchaser of your client's
property. You receive no compensation from the purchaser of the
property.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits public officials from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest. A "public official" is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. As a member of the Plumas County Board of
Supervisors, you are a public official subject to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.

In furtherance of the provisions of Section 87100, Section
87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally, on the official or on a member of his or
her immediate family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * *

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official

Our File No. I-92-600
Page 3


within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

               Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).


     You are the proprietor of an engineering firm, and the firm
is a business entity in which you have an investment interest.
(Section 82005.) You stated in our telephone conversation of
February 17, 1993, that the gross receipts of the firm are
approximately $30,000 annually.3  You also stated that your firm
has received approximately $5,000 from your client in the
preceding twelve months.  Therefore, you must disqualify yourself
from participating in any decision of the Plumas County Board of
Supervisors which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your engineering firm or your client.

Foreseeability

     The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.  To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required.  (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.)  The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.  (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

Materiality

     You have not provided us with any facts pertaining to a
decision pending before the Plumas County Board of Supervisors.
However, you anticipate that the purchaser of the property in
question will request approval of development plans which you
would have reviewed on behalf of the seller of the property.
If you determine that it is foreseeable that a decision of the
Plumas County Board of Supervisors will have a material financial
effect on you, your engineering firm, or your client, your
disqualification is required.

     The Commission has adopted regulations to determine whether
the financial effect of a decision on an official's economic

_______________

     3  Income also includes a pro-rata share of any income of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or
greater.

Our File No. I-92-600
Page 4


interests will be material.  When an official's economic interests
are directly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.1
establishes a presumption that the effect will be material.⁴

     Under Regulation 18702.1(a)(1), the effect of a decision is
material if there is a nexus between the purpose for which the
official receives income and the governmental decision.
Subdivision (d) of the regulation states there is a nexus between
the purpose for which an official receives income and a
governmental decision if the official receives income to achieve a
goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or
hindered by the decision.  We have consistently interpreted this
regulation to mean that a public official may not accomplish as an
official what he or she has been paid to do in his or her non-
governmental capacity.  (Silver Advice Letter, No. I-90-644,
Preers Advice Letter, No. A-86-281, and Shaw Advice Letter,
No. I-87-114.)  While the nexus test is fact-based and cannot be
applied in the abstract, it would appear that there would be a
nexus between your actions on the board of supervisors and your
private duties as an engineer.

     Accordingly, it would also appear you would not be able to
participate in decisions regarding the approval or denial of the
development plans.  However, you would be able to have necessary
contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation
of the drawings or submissions which you have personally prepared.
(Regulation 18700.1(b)(4) and Huguet Advice Letter, No. I-87-245.)

     You and your client may also be affected indirectly by
decisions before the Plumas County Board of Supervisors.
Regulations 18702.2-18702.6 provide the general standards for
determining materiality.

     If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision of the Plumas
County Board of Supervisors will affect your engineering firm or
your client, your disqualification will be required unless the
effect of the decision will not be distinguishable from the effect
of the decision on the public generally.

     For your convenience, we are enclosing a general discussion
of the "public generally" exception and other pertinent
regulations.




⁴ An economic interest is directly involved in a decision when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named
party in, or the subject of, the proceeding.  (Regulation
18702.1(b).)

Our File No. I-92-600
Page 5


We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.5

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures




5 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
